top_categories.workout_context: 17,717ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
At mile 60 of 105, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
L'Etape du Tour de France's alpine mountain passes forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 105 miles.
Race-pace efforts that mimic L'Etape du Tour de France's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before July.
168ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 105 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
At L'Etape du Tour de France, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
17,717ft of climbing on grades that change every minute. You need to switch from seated to standing without losing rhythm.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of L'Etape du Tour de France. Train it until it feels boring.
How long can you hold 105% FTP before you blow? At L'Etape du Tour de France, that number decides whether you bridge or get dropped.
Short, max efforts are unavoidable. Punchy climbs, gap closures, attacks on alpine mountain passes. If you haven't trained them, you'll crack.
105 miles rewards fat oxidation. This volume work shifts your fuel mix so you're still burning fat at hour 5, not bonking at hour 3.

race_overlay.heat: July in Bourg d'Oisans → Alpe d'Huez (via Col de Sarenne) can bring heat. Complete 3–4 heat exposure sessions in the final 2 weeks before L'Etape du Tour de France. Increase sodium intake 48 hours before race day.
race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for L'Etape du Tour de France’s 105 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.
race_overlay.altitude: L'Etape du Tour de France with 17,717ft of climbing, much of it above 8,000ft demands altitude preparation. Arrive 5–7 days early for acclimatization. Expect 5–15% power reduction at altitude. Increase iron intake 4 weeks out. Hydrate aggressively — altitude increases fluid loss by 20–40%.
race_overlay.terrain: Highly technical terrain at L'Etape du Tour de France demands practice on similar surfaces. Ride alpine mountain passes at race-day cadence weekly. Practice cornering, descending, and power delivery on unstable surfaces. Dial in tire pressure before race week — 5 PSI wrong costs you 15+ minutes over 105 miles.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on VO2max, Durability, and Gravel Specific to prepare you for 105 miles of alpine mountain passes in Bourg d'Oisans → Alpe d'Huez (via Col de Sarenne).
